context_dialogue.text: Good idea. And don't **forget to include** your research projects.

context_dialogue.speaker: Marc
context_dialogue.text: Right. I also **hope to get** an interview. I'd **love to study** abroad.

noticing_prompts: Todos los verbos resaltados van seguidos de 'to + verbo'. ¿Cuáles son?
¿Qué diferencia hay con los verbos de la lección anterior (gerundio)? ¿Puedes sustituir 'want to apply' por 'want applying'?
¿Hay algún patrón temático en estos verbos? ¿Hablan más del futuro/intención o de actividades actuales/pasadas?
rules.explanation: Algunos verbos siempre van seguidos de **to + infinitivo**. Los más comunes: **want, need, decide, hope, plan, expect, agree, refuse, manage, offer, promise, forget, learn, choose, seem, tend, afford, fail, pretend**. Muchos de estos expresan intención, decisión, o actitud hacia una acción futura. A diferencia del gerundio (que mira hacia lo que ya existe/pasado), el infinitivo a menudo mira hacia el futuro.

structured_input.sentence: She agreed to help with the event.
structured_input.question: ¿Cuál es la estructura después de 'agree'?
structured_input.feedback: Agree siempre va con to + infinitivo. 'She agreed helping' = incorrecto. Otros verbos similares: promise, refuse, offer, decide.

structured_input.sentence: They managed to solve the problem in time.
structured_input.question: ¿Qué significa 'managed to + infinitivo'?
structured_input.feedback: Manage to + infinitivo = lograr/conseguir hacer algo (implica que no era fácil). 'They managed to solve it' = lograron resolverlo finalmente.

structured_input.sentence: I can't afford to buy a new car right now.
structured_input.question: ¿Qué expresa 'afford to' aquí?
options: Tener los recursos (dinero, tiempo) para hacer algo
structured_input.feedback: Afford to = tener suficiente dinero/tiempo para hacer algo. 'Can't afford to' = no tener los medios para.

quiz.sentence: She decided ___ (change) her career after the pandemic.
quiz.feedback_why: Decide siempre + to + infinitivo. Expresa una decisión consciente sobre el futuro.
quiz.contrast: 'She decided changing her career' = incorrecto. Decide nunca va con gerundio.

quiz.sentence: I forgot ___ (lock) the door. I have to go back.
quiz.feedback_why: Forget + to-infinitivo = olvidar hacer algo (la acción no se hizo). 'I forgot to lock' = no lo cerré con llave.
quiz.contrast: 'I forgot locking the door.' = olvidé el recuerdo de haber cerrado la puerta (la acción sí ocurrió). Forget/remember cambian de significado con gerundio vs infinitivo.

quiz.sentence: Do you think he'll manage ___ (finish) on time?
quiz.feedback_why: Manage + to + infinitivo = conseguir/lograr. Siempre infinitivo.
quiz.contrast: 'Manage finishing' = incorrecto. Manage only takes to-infinitive.

quiz.sentence: We can't afford ___ (make) any more mistakes.
quiz.feedback_why: Afford + to + infinitivo. Aquí en sentido figurado: no nos podemos permitir el lujo de.
quiz.contrast: Afford siempre infinitivo. 'Can't afford making mistakes' = incorrecto.

quiz.sentence: She promised ___ (call) when she arrived.
quiz.feedback_why: Promise + to + infinitivo. Promesa sobre acción futura → infinitivo.
quiz.contrast: 'She promised calling' = incorrecto. Promise, agree, refuse, offer siempre van con to + infinitivo.

rules.title: Prepositions of Time: at, on, in
rules.title_es: Preposiciones de tiempo: at, on, in

context_dialogue.speaker: Mia
context_dialogue.text: The meeting is **on** Monday **at** 9 a.m.

context_dialogue.speaker: Dan
context_dialogue.text: Can we move it to Tuesday? I have a dentist appointment **in** the morning.

context_dialogue.speaker: Mia
context_dialogue.text: Sure. What about **at** 10?

context_dialogue.speaker: Dan
context_dialogue.text: Perfect. And the deadline is **in** March, right?

context_dialogue.speaker: Mia
context_dialogue.text: Yes, **on** the 15th. We have plenty of time.

noticing_prompts.q: Which preposition is used before a time of day (e.g., 9 a.m.)? Write an example from the dialogue.
Which preposition goes before days (Monday, Tuesday) and dates (the 15th)?
Which preposition is used before months and parts of the day (morning, evening)?

rules.explanation:
Use **at** for specific times: *at 9 a.m., at noon, at midnight*.
Use **on** for days and dates: *on Monday, on the 15th, on Christmas Day*.
Use **in** for months, years, seasons, and parts of the day: *in March, in 2024, in winter, in the morning*.

> **Quick rule:** at = clock, on = calendar day, in = longer period.

structured_input.sentence: I was born ___ 1998.
structured_input.question: Which preposition correctly fills the blank?
structured_input.feedback: Use **in** before years: *in 1998*.

structured_input.sentence: The party starts ___ 8 p.m. ___ Saturday.
structured_input.question: Which pair of prepositions is correct?
structured_input.feedback: **at** for a clock time, **on** for a day of the week.

structured_input.sentence: She usually goes to the gym ___ the afternoon.
structured_input.question: Which preposition is correct?
structured_input.feedback: Use **in** for parts of the day: *in the afternoon*. Exception: at night.

quiz.sentence: The conference is ___ Monday morning.
quiz.feedback_why: Use **on** before days of the week.
quiz.contrast: Say *on Monday* (day) but *in the morning* (part of day).

quiz.sentence: I always drink tea ___ night.
quiz.feedback_why: **at night** is a fixed expression — the only part-of-day that uses *at*, not *in*.
quiz.contrast: Compare: *in the morning / afternoon / evening* but **at night**.

quiz.feedback_why: Use **in** before years and months.
quiz.contrast: *in 2019, in November* — both use **in** because they are longer time periods.

quiz.sentence: The deadline is ___ the 30th.
quiz.feedback_why: Use **on** before specific dates.
quiz.contrast: *on the 30th* (date) vs *in March* (month).

quiz.sentence: The train leaves ___ exactly noon.
quiz.feedback_why: Use **at** for exact clock times and reference points like noon and midnight.
quiz.contrast: *at noon, at midnight, at 3 o'clock* — all use **at**.

rules.title: Prepositions of Place: at, on, in
rules.title_es: Preposiciones de lugar: at, on, in

context_dialogue.speaker: Leo
context_dialogue.text: Where are you? I'm **at** the coffee shop on Main Street.

context_dialogue.speaker: Sara
context_dialogue.text: I'm still **at** home. I'll be there in ten minutes.

context_dialogue.speaker: Leo
context_dialogue.text: I'm sitting **at** the table **in** the corner, **on** the left side.

context_dialogue.speaker: Sara
context_dialogue.text: Got it. Is there a parking lot **in** the building?

context_dialogue.speaker: Leo
context_dialogue.text: Yes, it's **on** the ground floor.

noticing_prompts.q: Leo says he is 'at the coffee shop' and 'at the table'. What kind of locations use 'at'?
Which preposition appears before 'the corner' and 'the building'? What does it suggest about these spaces?
How is 'on' used for location in this dialogue? Write the example.

rules.explanation:
Use **at** for specific points or locations: *at the door, at the station, at work, at home*.
Use **in** for enclosed spaces or areas: *in the room, in the city, in the building, in the corner*.
Use **on** for surfaces and floors: *on the table, on the wall, on the second floor, on the left*.

> **Tip:** at = point, in = enclosed, on = surface.

structured_input.sentence: There is a supermarket ___ the corner.
structured_input.question: Which preposition is correct?
structured_input.feedback: Use **at** for a specific point or location like a corner.

structured_input.sentence: She left her keys ___ the table.
structured_input.question: Which preposition is correct?
structured_input.feedback: Use **on** for surfaces: the keys are resting on the table's surface.

structured_input.sentence: The meeting room is ___ the third floor.
structured_input.question: Which preposition is correct?
structured_input.feedback: Use **on** for floors of a building: *on the third floor*.

quiz.sentence: I'll meet you ___ the entrance.
quiz.feedback_why: Use **at** for a specific meeting point or location.
quiz.contrast: *at the entrance* (a point) vs *in the building* (enclosed space).

quiz.sentence: She lives ___ a small village.
quiz.feedback_why: Use **in** for enclosed or contained areas: villages, cities, regions.
quiz.contrast: *in a village, in Madrid, in the south* — all enclosed areas use **in**.

quiz.sentence: Your coat is ___ the chair.
quiz.feedback_why: The coat rests on the surface of the chair — use **on**.
quiz.contrast: *on the chair* (surface) vs *in the closet* (enclosed).

quiz.sentence: He works ___ a hospital.
quiz.feedback_why: Use **in** for buildings and institutions when referring to the space inside.
quiz.contrast: *in the hospital* (inside) vs *at the hospital* (at that location/address).

quiz.sentence: There is a photo ___ the wall.
quiz.feedback_why: The photo is attached to the surface of the wall — use **on**.
quiz.contrast: *on the wall* (surface) vs *in the room* (enclosed area).

rules.title: Verb + Preposition Collocations

context_dialogue.speaker: Ana
context_dialogue.text: I've been thinking **about** changing careers.

context_dialogue.speaker: Tom
context_dialogue.text: Really? What are you interested **in**?

context_dialogue.speaker: Ana
context_dialogue.text: I've always dreamed **of** working in design. But I'm worried **about** starting over.

context_dialogue.speaker: Tom
context_dialogue.text: I believe **in** you. You should apply **for** that design course.

context_dialogue.speaker: Ana
context_dialogue.text: You're right. I'll stop waiting **for** the perfect moment and just do it.

noticing_prompts.q: Find three different verb + preposition combinations in the dialogue. Write them here.
The adjective 'interested' uses 'in'. Can you think of two more adjectives or verbs that also take 'in'?
Both 'dream of' and 'worry about' are followed by -ing. What pattern do you notice about the structure verb + preposition + verb?

rules.explanation:
Many verbs and adjectives take a fixed preposition — you need to learn these as collocations:

**Common verb + preposition pairs:**
- think **about** / dream **of** / worry **about**
- apply **for** / wait **for** / ask **for**
- believe **in** / succeed **in** / specialise **in**
- depend **on** / rely **on**
- listen **to** / belong **to** / apologise **to** someone

> There is no single grammatical rule — these must be memorised as fixed expressions. After a preposition, a verb takes the **-ing** form: *think about going, dream of working*.

structured_input.sentence: She is really good ___ public speaking.
structured_input.question: Which preposition collocates with 'good'?
structured_input.feedback: **good at** something is a fixed collocation. *She is good at tennis.*

structured_input.sentence: They decided to apply ___ the job.
structured_input.question: Which preposition correctly follows 'apply'?
structured_input.feedback: **apply for** a job/grant/position. *She applied for the scholarship.*

structured_input.sentence: I really believe ___ second chances.
structured_input.question: Which preposition follows 'believe' when talking about principles or ideas?
structured_input.feedback: **believe in** something = to have faith in it. *I believe in hard work.*

quiz.sentence: She's been thinking ___ moving abroad.
quiz.feedback_why: **think about** something is the standard collocation for considering an idea.
quiz.contrast: You can also *think of* something, but *think about* is more common for ongoing consideration.

quiz.sentence: He apologised ___ being late.
quiz.feedback_why: **apologise for** doing something. The preposition 'for' introduces the reason.
quiz.contrast: *apologise for* an action vs *apologise to* a person: 'He apologised to his boss for being late.'

quiz.sentence: I'm not very good ___ cooking.
quiz.feedback_why: **good at** is a fixed collocation. It cannot be replaced by another preposition.
quiz.contrast: *good at* (skill) vs *good for* (beneficial): 'Exercise is good for you.'

quiz.sentence: Can I rely ___ you to be there on time?
quiz.feedback_why: **rely on** someone/something = to depend on them. Fixed collocation.
quiz.contrast: *rely on* vs *depend on* — both mean the same and take **on**.

quiz.sentence: They're waiting ___ the results.
quiz.feedback_why: **wait for** something/someone is the standard collocation.
quiz.contrast: Compare: *wait for* (expecting) vs *wait on* (serve): 'The waiter waited on the customers.'

rules.title: Common Phrasal Verbs

context_dialogue.speaker: Jake
context_dialogue.text: Can you **turn down** the music? I'm trying to work.

context_dialogue.speaker: Amy
context_dialogue.text: Sure! I didn't realise it was so loud. I'll **turn it down** right now.

context_dialogue.speaker: Jake
context_dialogue.text: Thanks. Also, did you **find out** about the Wi-Fi password?

context_dialogue.speaker: Amy
context_dialogue.text: Not yet. I'll **look it up** online. Or we can just ask the receptionist.

context_dialogue.speaker: Jake
context_dialogue.text: Good idea. I have to **log in** to the work server before noon.

noticing_prompts.q: What is the difference between 'turn down the music' and 'turn it down'? What changes when a pronoun is used?
'Find out' and 'look up' both mean to discover information. Can you think of a one-word verb with the same meaning?
Write two phrasal verbs from the dialogue that use the particle 'down'. What does 'down' add to the meaning?

rules.explanation:
A **phrasal verb** is a verb + particle (preposition or adverb) that creates a new meaning:
- **turn down** = reduce / refuse
- **find out** = discover
- **look up** = search for information
- **log in** = connect to a system

**Word order with object pronouns:**
With separable phrasal verbs, pronouns go *between* the verb and particle:
- Correct: *Turn it down.* / *Turn down the music.*
- Wrong: *Turn down it.*

> If the object is a pronoun (it, them, him), it **must** go in the middle.

structured_input.sentence: She ___ the offer because the salary was too low.
structured_input.question: Which phrasal verb means 'refused'?
structured_input.feedback: **Turn down** an offer = refuse it. *She turned down the job offer.*

structured_input.sentence: I need to ___ the meeting time — can you check the email?
structured_input.question: Which phrasal verb means 'discover'?
structured_input.feedback: **Find out** = discover information. *Find out the time / find out what happened.*

structured_input.sentence: I don't know what this word means. I'll ___ in the dictionary.
structured_input.question: Which option is grammatically correct (pronoun placement)?
structured_input.feedback: With pronouns, the object goes between verb and particle: **look it up**, not *look up it*.

quiz.sentence: He ___ smoking after his doctor's advice.
quiz.feedback_why: **Give up** = stop doing something (a habit or activity).
quiz.contrast: *give up* (stop a habit) vs *turn down* (refuse an offer).

quiz.sentence: Can you ___ the volume? I can barely hear it.
quiz.feedback_why: **Turn up** = increase the volume or level. Opposite of *turn down*.
quiz.contrast: *turn up* (increase) vs *turn down* (reduce or refuse).

quiz.sentence: She ___ a great cafe near the office.
quiz.feedback_why: **Come across** = find or meet by chance. *I came across a great article.*
quiz.contrast: *come across* (by chance) vs *look for* (intentional search).

quiz.sentence: Don't forget to ___ before you leave the computer.
quiz.feedback_why: **Log out / log off** = end a computer session. Opposite of *log in*.
quiz.contrast: *log in* (start session) vs *log out* (end session).

quiz.sentence: The meeting was ___ because the manager was ill.
quiz.feedback_why: **Put off** = postpone or delay something.
quiz.contrast: *put off* (delay) vs *call off* (cancel completely).

rules.title: Separable vs Inseparable Phrasal Verbs

context_dialogue.speaker: Lena
context_dialogue.text: I need to **fill in** this form. Can you help me?

context_dialogue.speaker: Mark
context_dialogue.text: Sure. Just **fill it in** and I'll check it afterward.

context_dialogue.speaker: Lena
context_dialogue.text: Thanks. Also, we need to **deal with** the complaint from the client.

context_dialogue.speaker: Mark
context_dialogue.text: I'll **look into** it today. We can't just **put it off**.

context_dialogue.speaker: Lena
context_dialogue.text: Agreed. Let's **go over** the report together at three.

noticing_prompts.q: Compare 'fill in this form' and 'fill it in'. What rule applies when a pronoun replaces the noun?
The dialogue says 'deal with the complaint' and 'look into it'. Can we say 'deal the complaint with' or 'look it into'? Why not?
Which phrasal verbs in the dialogue do you think are inseparable (cannot be split)? List them.

rules.explanation:
**Separable** phrasal verbs can have their object in the middle or at the end:
- *fill in the form* / *fill the form in* / *fill it in* — all correct
- Wrong: *fill in it* — pronoun must go in the middle

**Inseparable** phrasal verbs always keep the verb and particle together:
- *look into the problem* — correct
- *deal with the complaint* — correct
- Wrong: *look the problem into* / *deal the complaint with*

**Common inseparable:** *look into, deal with, come across, go over, run into, look after*

> **Memory tip:** If the particle is a preposition (into, with, after), the verb is almost always inseparable.

structured_input.sentence: She _____ the children while their parents were away.
structured_input.question: Which phrasal verb means 'took care of'?
structured_input.feedback: **Look after** = take care of. It is inseparable: *look after the children*, never *look the children after*.

structured_input.sentence: I ran ___ my old teacher at the supermarket.
structured_input.question: Which particle completes this inseparable phrasal verb?
structured_input.feedback: **Run into** someone = meet them unexpectedly. It is inseparable.

structured_input.sentence: Can you _____ this document before the meeting?
structured_input.question: Which option correctly uses a separable phrasal verb meaning 'review'?
structured_input.feedback: **Go over** = review. With pronouns: *go over it* is the natural form.

quiz.sentence: She ___ her younger brother every day after school.
quiz.feedback_why: **Look after** = care for someone. This is an inseparable phrasal verb.
quiz.contrast: *look after* (care for) vs *look into* (investigate).

quiz.sentence: Please ___ the contract carefully before you sign.
quiz.feedback_why: **Go over** = review or check something carefully.
quiz.contrast: *go over* (review) vs *go through* (examine step by step).

quiz.sentence: The manager ___ the problem immediately.
quiz.feedback_why: **Look into** = investigate or examine. It is inseparable.
quiz.contrast: *look into* (investigate) vs *look after* (care for).

quiz.sentence: He ___ an old friend at the airport.
quiz.feedback_why: **Run into** = meet someone by chance. It is inseparable.
quiz.contrast: *run into* (meet by chance) vs *come across* (find by chance).

quiz.sentence: I need to ___ this application form.
quiz.feedback_why: **Fill in / fill out** = complete a form. Both are separable: *fill it in* or *fill in the form*.
quiz.contrast: *fill in* and *fill out* are interchangeable. With pronouns: *fill it in*, not *fill in it*.

rules.title: Idiomatic Phrasal Verbs

context_dialogue.speaker: Eva
context_dialogue.text: I tried to **bring up** the budget issue in the meeting, but no one listened.

context_dialogue.speaker: Sam
context_dialogue.text: That topic always **comes up** at the worst times.

context_dialogue.speaker: Eva
context_dialogue.text: I know. I didn't want to **fall out** with my boss over it, so I just let it go.

context_dialogue.speaker: Sam
context_dialogue.text: Smart. Sometimes you have to **put up with** things you don't like.

context_dialogue.speaker: Eva
context_dialogue.text: True. But if it happens again, I'm going to **stand up for** myself.

noticing_prompts.q: 'Bring up' means to introduce a topic. Can you think of another meaning of 'bring up'?
'Put up with' has three parts: put + up + with. What does the whole phrase mean? Can you split it?
noticing_prompts.placeholder: It means... and it is inseparable...

noticing_prompts.q: What is the difference in meaning between 'let it go' (line 3) and 'stand up for' (line 5)?
noticing_prompts.placeholder: Let it go = ..., stand up for = ...

rules.explanation:
Some phrasal verbs are **idiomatic** — their meaning cannot be guessed from the individual words:

- **bring up** = introduce a topic / raise a child
- **come up** = appear / be mentioned
- **fall out** (with sb) = have an argument and stop being friendly
- **let go** = release / stop worrying about something
- **put up with** = tolerate something unpleasant
- **stand up for** = defend or support yourself or others

> **Three-word phrasal verbs** like *put up with* and *stand up for* are always inseparable.

structured_input.sentence: She's incredibly patient — she can ___ a lot of stress.
structured_input.question: Which phrasal verb means 'tolerate'?
structured_input.feedback: **Put up with** = tolerate. It's a three-part inseparable phrasal verb.

structured_input.sentence: He always ___ his colleagues when they're treated unfairly.
structured_input.question: Which phrasal verb means 'defends'?
structured_input.feedback: **Stand up for** = defend or support someone or a principle.

structured_input.sentence: An interesting problem ___ during the meeting.
structured_input.question: Which phrasal verb means 'appeared or was mentioned'?
structured_input.feedback: **Come up** = appear or be mentioned (of a topic, problem, or issue).

quiz.sentence: I don't want to ___ with my flatmate over something so trivial.
quiz.feedback_why: **Fall out (with someone)** = have an argument that damages the relationship.
quiz.contrast: *fall out with* (argue and drift apart) vs *fall out* (physically fall) — context makes it clear.

quiz.sentence: Don't worry about what happened — just ___ and move on.
quiz.feedback_why: **Let it go** = stop holding onto a worry, grudge, or mistake.
quiz.contrast: *let it go* (stop worrying) vs *let go of* (physically release something).

quiz.sentence: She ___ her three children alone after her husband left.
quiz.feedback_why: **Bring up** children = raise them. This is a separable, idiomatic use.
quiz.contrast: *bring up* a child (raise) vs *bring up* a topic (introduce) — same verb, two meanings.

quiz.sentence: You should always ___ what you believe in.
quiz.feedback_why: **Stand up for** something or someone = defend it or them. Always three words.
quiz.contrast: *stand up for* (defend) vs *stand up to* (resist/confront): 'He stood up to his bullies.'

quiz.sentence: I cannot ___ the noise from the construction next door any longer.
quiz.feedback_why: **Put up with** = tolerate something unpleasant. Always three words, always inseparable.
quiz.contrast: *put up with* (tolerate) vs *put up* (erect/hang): 'We put up a shelf.'
